script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Morning, Sarah. Is it July 24th already? Wow.
script.ja: おはようございます、サラさん。もう7月24日ですか？すごいな。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It is. Good morning, Alex.
script.ja: そうね。アレックス、おはよう。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Feels like the week just started, but it's Thursday already. And it's another rainy one out there.
script.ja: まだ週が始まったばかりな気がするのに、もう木曜日。そして、また雨ですね。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Yeah, slight rain. They're saying 88% chance all day. And 34 degrees. It's really humid.
script.ja: ええ、小雨ね。一日中88％の降水確率だって。34度。本当に蒸し暑い。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Right? I'm already sweating, and I haven't even had my coffee yet.
script.ja: でしょう？もう汗だくですよ、まだコーヒーも飲んでないのに。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Get that coffee.
script.ja: コーヒー飲みなさい。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: I will. Did you see that news from Nagoya? About the man who fell from the apartment building?
script.ja: そうします。名古屋のニュース見ました？マンションから男性が落ちたっていう。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: I did. That was terrible. For both of them, really.
script.ja: 見たわ。ひどい話ね。どちらにとっても本当に。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah, I can't imagine. And hitting someone walking below... just unbelievable. I heard the police had to cordon off the whole street.
script.ja: ええ、想像できないです。そして下に歩いてた人にぶつかるなんて...信じられない。警察が通り全体を『cordon off』したって聞きました。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: They did. It was a big area.
script.ja: そうね。広い範囲だったわ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: What does 'cordon' mean, actually? Like, block it off?
script.ja: ところで、『cordon』ってどういう意味なんですか？立ち入り禁止にする、みたいな？

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Exactly. To block off an area, usually with police tape or ropes, to control access.
script.ja: その通り。警察のテープやロープなどで、人々の立ち入りを制限するためにエリアを封鎖することよ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Ah, I see. My building manager is so strict, I feel like they'd cordon off the laundry room if someone left socks in there.
script.ja: なるほど。うちのマンションの管理人、すごく厳しいから、誰かが靴下を置きっぱなしにしたらランドリールームを封鎖しそうです。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Maybe.
script.ja: そうかもね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Anyway... I was looking at my phone on the train. You know, what day it is today? It's 'Kakigori Day' apparently!
script.ja: とにかく...電車の中で携帯を見てたんですけど。今日が何の日か知ってます？どうやら『かき氷の日』らしいです！

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Is it today? I thought that was tomorrow, the 25th.
script.ja: 今日？それは明日、25日だと思ってたけど。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Oh, is it tomorrow? My phone sometimes gets things wrong. But it said 'Natsu-go-ri', like 7-2-5. So maybe it is tomorrow.
script.ja: あ、明日ですか？僕の携帯、たまに間違えるんですよね。でも、『な（7）つ（2）ご（5）おり』って書いてあったんで、やっぱり明日なのかな。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It's because of the sound of 'natsu-gori' – 7 for 'na', 2 for 'tsu', 5 for 'go'. And also the hottest day on record back in 1933, 40.8 degrees.
script.ja: 『なつごおり』の音にちなんでるのよ。7が『な』、2が『つ』、5が『ご』。それと、1933年に40.8度という日本最高気温を記録した日でもあるから。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Wow, 40.8 degrees. That's really hot. I wish I had a kakigori right now. Or maybe a cold coffee. My little one is sleeping so badly these days, I just need caffeine.
script.ja: うわ、40.8度。それは本当に暑いですね。今すぐかき氷食べたいな。もしくは冷たいコーヒー。うちの小さいのが最近全然寝てくれなくて、とにかくカフェインが必要です。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: I remember those nights. You just get used to being tired.
script.ja: あの頃を思い出すわ。ただ疲れていることに慣れるだけよ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: I'm trying. But she just started pulling herself up, so she's always trying to climb out of the crib now. It's a whole new level of exhaustion.
script.ja: 頑張ってますけどね。最近、つかまり立ちを始めたから、いつもベビーベッドから這い出そうとしてるんです。疲労のレベルがまた一段上がりました。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: They do that. Speaking of summer, did you see the news about swimming beaches? They're down 30% in Japan over the last 30 years.
script.ja: そういうものよ。夏といえば、海水浴場のニュース見た？この30年で日本中の海水浴場が30%減ったって。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Really? Wow. I guess more people just go to pools now? Or stay home with air conditioning.
script.ja: 本当ですか？へえ。やっぱり、みんな今はプールに行くんでしょうか？それかエアコンの効いた家で過ごすとか。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Could be. Or maybe the quality of the water, or just less interest in maintaining them. It's a shame, though. I love going to the beach. Not for swimming, really, but just to relax by the water.
script.ja: そうかもしれないわね。水質の問題とか、あるいは維持管理への関心が薄れたとか。でも残念だわ。私はビーチに行くのが好きなの。泳ぐためじゃなくて、ただ水辺でリラックスするのが。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah, it's nice. We haven't taken the baby to the beach yet. Too much sand, I think. She'd just eat it.
script.ja: ええ、いいですよね。うちはまだ赤ちゃんをビーチに連れて行ってないんです。砂が多すぎると思うし。たぶん全部食べちゃうでしょうね。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: They probably would. I want to go to Okinawa next year, maybe try one of the smaller islands. The ones with really clear water and beautiful foliage near the shore.
script.ja: そうね、きっとそうするわ。来年、沖縄に行きたいな。たぶん小さな島のどれかに行ってみる。本当に透明な水と、岸辺の美しい『foliage』があるようなところ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Okinawa sounds great. What's 'foliage'?
script.ja: 沖縄いいですね。『foliage』って何ですか？

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It's all the leaves and plants on trees. The green part of nature, especially a lot of it together.
script.ja: 木々の葉っぱや植物のことよ。自然の緑の部分、特にたくさん集まっているもの。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Ah, like the jungle-y bits! Got it. That sounds nice. My boss was really brusque with me yesterday, I need a vacation.
script.ja: ああ、ジャングルっぽいところ！なるほど。それはいいですね。昨日、上司が僕にすごく『brusque』で、休暇が必要です。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Oh?
script.ja: あら？

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah, just, 'Do this, do that, now.' Really short. No explanation. Like he just woke up on the wrong side of the bed. What does 'brusque' mean again?
script.ja: ええ、もう、『これやれ、あれやれ、今すぐ。』って。本当に短くて、説明もなし。寝起きが悪かったみたいで。また、『brusque』ってどういう意味でしたっけ？

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It means speaking or behaving in a very direct, sudden, and often rude way. Not friendly.
script.ja: とても直接的で、突然で、しばしば無礼な話し方や行動のことよ。親しげじゃないの。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Exactly! That's it. It's tough when people are like that first thing in the morning. Especially before I've had my second coffee.
script.ja: まさにそれです！それだ。朝一番にそんな風な人だと大変ですよね。特に僕が2杯目のコーヒーを飲む前は。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Well, some mornings are harder than others, I guess. Did you see the news about the kanji character that has five different pronunciations?
script.ja: まあ、朝は日によって大変なこともあるわね。ところで、5つの異なる読み方がある漢字のニュース見た？

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Oh, yeah, I saw that. With a super simple sentence too. That's classic Japanese. Just when you think you understand it, there's another level of difficulty.
script.ja: ああ、見ました。すごくシンプルな文なのに、ってやつですよね。あれが日本の古典的なところですよ。理解したと思ったら、また別の難易度があるっていう。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: My son is struggling with kanji right now. He keeps saying Japanese is too hard.
script.ja: うちの息子も今、漢字に苦戦しているわ。日本語は難しすぎるっていつも言ってる。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Tell him to wait until he's trying to teach his own kid English. That's going to be a real challenge.
script.ja: 自分の子供に英語を教えることになったら、もっと大変だって言ってやってください。あれは本当に試練ですよ。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: True. Anyway, that's all the time we have for today on The Daily Tanu-chan Show.
script.ja: 確かに。さて、今日の『The Daily Tanu-chan Show』はここまでね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Thanks for listening, everyone. Have a good rest of your Thursday. And if you're eating kakigori, enjoy it for me!
script.ja: 皆さん、お聴きいただきありがとうございました。良い木曜日の残りの時間をお過ごしください。そして、かき氷を食べる方は、僕の分も楽しんでくださいね！

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: See you tomorrow.
script.ja: また明日。

news.title: Number of swimming beaches in Japan down 30% over 30 years
news.summary: The number of approved swimming beaches open during the summer in Japan has fallen by around 30 percent over the past three decades, a study by a travel…

wikipedia.title: かき氷の日
wikipedia.description: 日本かき氷協会が制定。かき氷の別名「夏氷・な（7）つ（2）ご（5）おり」の語呂合わせと、1933年（昭和8年）のこの日に山形市で、当時の日本最高気温40．8度を記録したことにちなんだもの。
